main_summary.summary: Mateo Camarillo, interviewed in San Diego by David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts his work building Chicano civic infrastructure in San Diego County from the 1960s through the 2000s. Born in Tijuana in 1941 to a U.S.-citizen mother who worked at Convair making B-29 bombers, Camarillo immigrated to San Diego in 1950, attended Herbert Hoover High School and San Diego State, then volunteered for the U.S. Army in 1965 and served in Germany as a Boy Scouts of America Transatlantic Council Scoutmaster. After graduate training at San Jose State and a Ford Foundation fellowship with Saul Alinsky in Chicago, he joined the San Diego County welfare department, founded the San Diego chapter of Trabajadores de la Raza, and won bilingual-pay bonuses at the county, city, and San Diego Gas and Electric. As executive director of the Chicano Federation (early 1970s), he sued San Diego County under federal Equal Employment Opportunity law in the longest-running civil rights employment case in U.S. history, opened the federation's offices under the San Diego-Coronado Bridge during the Chicano Park takeover led by his sister Tomasa Camarillo, secured General Revenue Sharing funds for community organizations, and built direct-line relationships with Mayor Pete Wilson and Police Chief Bill Kolender. He later founded Quetzal Bilingual Communications, owned McDonald's franchises and Spanish-language radio stations, helped draft the 1986 Immigration Reform and Control Act with Sen. Orrin Hatch, Sen. Edward Kennedy, and the National Council of La Raza, and served on multiple California redistricting bodies.
key_themes: Bilingual social services and pay equity
main_summary.historical_significance:
Camarillo's testimony documents a San Diego variant of the Chicano movement that emphasized institution-building, coalition with African American, Asian American, Filipino, and Native American organizations, and direct negotiation with municipal power rather than confrontational street tactics. His account of the San Diego County employment-discrimination lawsuit, the Chicano Park takeover led by his sister, and the formation of Trabajadores de la Raza as a national network of bilingual social workers provides a primary-source record of Mexican American civic organizing on the U.S.-Mexico border that has been less documented than the Los Angeles, San Antonio, and Crystal City movements.

The interview also preserves Camarillo's first-person account of immigration policy work with Sen. Orrin Hatch, Sen. Edward Kennedy, and President Ronald Reagan on the 1986 Immigration Reform and Control Act through the National Council of La Raza board, his Ford Foundation fellowship training under Saul Alinsky, and his work with March Fong Eu on statewide voter registration. The narrative connects the Chicano movement to broader civil rights litigation, federal revenue sharing, and the Greenlining Institute's multi-ethnic anti-redlining work in California.

chapters.title: Tijuana Birth and San Diego Immigration
chapters.summary: Camarillo introduces himself and recounts his 1941 birth in Tijuana to a U.S.-citizen mother who worked at Convair making B-29 bombers and at the Barrio Logan tuna factory during World War II. His family immigrated to San Diego in 1950 when he was 10. His mother Rufina was born in Campo, a San Diego County border community, and two of her brothers died in World War II.
related_events: World War II home-front industrial labor
Post-war Mexican immigration to San Diego

chapters.title: Schooling Without Bilingual Education
chapters.summary: Camarillo describes entering second grade in San Diego without English, failing his placement test, and asking classmates 'qué dijo' to translate the teacher. He recounts a teacher who invited him for a drink after school and humanized education for him. Sports and playground leadership became his path to English and self-confidence; he was placed in charge of junior patrols as a crossing guard.
related_events: Pre-Lau v. Nichols English-only public schooling

chapters.title: College, Kennedy, and Civic Awakening
chapters.summary: Camarillo wins two scholarships from Herbert Hoover High School and Allied Gardens, attends San Diego State, and graduates in four years. He hears President Kennedy's 1962 commencement address, joins the Viva Kennedy Clubs in San Diego, becomes a naturalized citizen at 21, and helps launch the Southwest Voter Registration Education Project as the first multicultural voter-registration drive in San Diego, including African American, Native American (14 county tribes), and Asian American voters.
related_events: Founding of Southwest Voter Registration Education Project

chapters.title: Army Service and the Jim Crow South
chapters.summary: Camarillo volunteers for the U.S. Army in 1965 and is sent to Georgia and South Carolina for basic training because spinal meningitis closed the California facilities. He recounts being refused service at a Charleston restaurant with an African American Georgian and a Puerto Rican soldier, and being denied entry to Atlanta night clubs that required 'membership cards' available only to whites. He describes integrated camaraderie within the Army across ethnic groups.

chapters.title: Germany, Boy Scouts, and Return to Social Work
chapters.summary: Sent to Germany as an infill for the 4th Armored Battalion rather than to Vietnam, Camarillo serves as a training officer doing a week's work in a day, then becomes Scoutmaster of the Boy Scouts of America Transatlantic Council, traveling across Europe with military-dependent children. The experience leads him to join the San Diego County welfare department on return to work with families as a bilingual social worker.

chapters.title: Trabajadores de la Raza and Bilingual Pay
chapters.summary: Facing a caseload of 600 Spanish-speaking families against the 60-family standard for English-only workers, Camarillo founds the San Diego chapter of Trabajadores de la Raza, building a national network with chapters in Los Angeles, San Jose, Houston, San Antonio, Denver, and New York. The organization wins a 10% bilingual pay bonus at San Diego County, then the city of San Diego, then San Diego Gas and Electric, and finally a statewide bilingual pay program with state assembly members Richard Alatorre and Mervyn Dymally.
related_events: Founding of Trabajadores de la Raza national network

chapters.title: Suing San Diego County for Employment Equity
chapters.summary: As executive director of the Chicano Federation in 1974, Camarillo organizes the six Latino attorneys in San Diego and county employees facing 67% rejection rates for Hispanic applicants. They sue San Diego County under federal Equal Employment Opportunity law in what Camarillo describes as the longest-lasting civil rights employment case in U.S. history. African American, Asian American, and Filipino plaintiffs join. The suit wins removal of artificial hiring barriers at the county, then sets precedent for the city and private sector.

chapters.title: Revenue Sharing Coalition and Anglo Allies
chapters.summary: When Nixon's General Revenue Sharing program threatens to fund only municipal pet projects, Camarillo and Chicano Federation chairman Dr. Alfredo Velasco coalition with the Community Congress, a largely Anglo organization rooted in the San Diego beach hippie movement. Together they travel to Washington, document congressional intent for low-income communities, sue the city and county, and secure over $5 million the first year and over $6 million the second year for community organizations bypassing local government.

chapters.title: Alinsky Training and San Jose Faculty
chapters.summary: Camarillo earns his master's in social work at San Diego State on the GI Bill while working for the county, then wins a Ford Foundation Fellowship to train under Saul Alinsky in Chicago. He joins the San Jose State faculty to start the school of social work as coordinator for field instruction and recruits Herman Gallegos, a National Council of La Raza founder who grew up alongside César Chávez in Sal Si Puedes (San Jose), to bring foundation-funding expertise.
keywords: San Jose State school of social work
related_events: Founding of National Council of La Raza

chapters.title: Chicano Park and Direct-Line Diplomacy
chapters.summary: Camarillo describes the Chicano Park takeover under the San Diego-Coronado Bridge, led by his sister Tomasa Camarillo as chairman of the Chicano Park Steering Committee, when the state reneged on a community-park promise and proposed a Highway Patrol station instead. Federally funded offices replaced the patrol station and housed the Brown Berets, Pinto Union (formerly incarcerated organizers), welfare services, and human resources. He recounts establishing direct phone-line relationships with Police Chief Bill Kolender and Mayor Pete Wilson to defuse incidents like the Brown Image low-rider beating without riots, and stopping a taxi-driver scheme that delivered Mexican-looking residents to immigration.

chapters.title: McDonald's, Radio, and Civic Independence
chapters.summary: Recognizing he could not keep suing institutions that funded the Chicano Federation, Camarillo leaves in 1976 for the private sector. After two McDonald's franchise rejections, he arrives at his third meeting with elected officials Dean Kennerberg knew, and wins approval. He partners with Secretary of State March Fong Eu on a free-french-fries voter incentive, founds Quetzal Bilingual Communications, applies under Reagan-era airwave deregulation for a 1040 AM Des Moines-derived West Coast frequency, and builds half a dozen Spanish-language radio stations linked by satellite. His station broadcasts Port Commission warnings about Monsanto and Harvest at Sea toxic releases over Barrio Logan.
related_events: California Secretary of State voter registration initiative

chapters.title: PACs, Patronage, and Hispanic Judges
chapters.summary: Camarillo organizes McDonald's franchisees into MacPAC and contributes to Jerry Brown, Gray Davis, Cruz Bustamante, and Pete Wilson through the Golden 500 (a $500-membership Hispanic political fundraising network with chapters in San Diego, Los Angeles, San Francisco, and Sacramento). When a six-judge vacancy opened in San Diego under Brown, Camarillo nominated six Hispanic attorneys and all six were appointed. He notes Brown's relationship with Linda Ronstadt and contrasts Pete Wilson's later anti-immigrant gubernatorial turn with the 20-year absence of statewide Republican wins in California (except Arnold Schwarzenegger).

chapters.title: La Raza Cósmica and Multicultural Curriculum
chapters.summary: Camarillo links the term 'La Raza' to José Vasconcelos's post-revolutionary book La Raza Cósmica and names his companies after Mesoamerican civilizations (Quetzal, Quinto Sol, Aztlán). He describes his college teacher's expertise on the Aztecs, Mayans, Toltecs, and Chichimecs as the foundation of his commitment to multicultural curriculum and naming practices, framing knowledge of other cultures as the sign of an educated person rather than a concession to ethnic interest.

chapters.title: Immigration Reform and the Greenlining Institute
chapters.summary: Serving the maximum terms on the National Council of La Raza board, Camarillo works with Sen. Orrin Hatch, Sen. Edward Kennedy, and Reagan administration delegates on the 1986 Immigration Reform and Control Act. He becomes the San Diego delegate to the Greenlining Institute board for over 20 years, chairs the Public Utilities Commission's deregulation of the California telephone industry for 10 years, and helps build a citizens' board providing services in 10 languages across all wire and wireless companies in California.

chapters.title: Electoral Campaigns and Redistricting
chapters.summary: Camarillo describes three losing races for elected office, San Diego Unified school board (defeated when 60% of district voters were senior citizens interested in hot lunches rather than education), Southwestern Community College board (split-vote loss against a wealthy Coronado opponent after a second Hispanic entered the race), and the newly-created 9th San Diego City Council district, where less-than-10% Latino turnout favored an incumbent television newscaster. He recounts serving on California State Senate redistricting in 1980, San Diego County redistricting, and the 2010 Latino Redistricting Committee that produced the city's first Latino-majority district.

chapters.title: Immigration Reflections and Closing
chapters.summary: Camarillo and his wife now operate a breathalyzer-installation business for drivers with DUI citations, working with a German manufacturer with World War I gas expertise. He closes by reflecting on immigration: the U.S. as a country of immigrants, the strawberry fields of Oregon's Willamette Valley that closed schools early so children could harvest, the 80% immigrant share of California farm work, and the 1986 Immigration Reform and Control Act's pardon framework. He critiques wall-building proposals from the 2016 presidential campaign and calls for regulated immigration without exclusion.
related_events: Reagan-era Immigration Reform and Control Act

loc_video.caption: Mateo Camarillo oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in National City, California, 2016 June 28.
